74	## What Happens During a Sync
75	
76	`pc-switcher sync <target>` runs a fixed sequence of steps. The order matters: each step sets up the environment the next one depends on. All steps run on the **source** machine, acting on the **target** over SSH.
77	
78	The sequence stops at the first failure, and cleanup always runs: release locks, kill remote processes, close the connection.
79	
80	The ten logical phases are listed below. The live UI numbers steps dynamically, because the run-jobs phase expands to one step per enabled sync job. With the default two jobs (`folder_sync`, then `vscode_state_sync`), a sync runs as 11 steps, ending with the post-sync snapshots.
81	
82	1. **Acquire source lock.** Local lock file; this machine cannot join any other sync (as source or target) while this one runs.
83	2. **Establish SSH connection.** Creates the local and remote executors every later step uses. Nothing touches the target before this point.
84	3. **Acquire target lock.** A persistent remote process holds the same unified lock on the target; released during cleanup.
85	   - *Between steps 3 and 4 (not a progress step): out-of-order check.* Detects cases where the target may hold independent state — no prior sync history, the target last synced with a different machine, or this machine pushing again without a back-sync first. Warns and asks for confirmation (never a hard abort, since re-syncing the same direction is a legitimate workflow). Skip with `--allow-out-of-order`; in `--dry-run` the warning is logged and the sync continues.
86	4. **Discover & validate jobs.**
87	   - Load enabled jobs from config
88	   - Validate their config
89	   - Run each job's `validate()` against live system state: checks `sudo rsync` availability, `acl` package installation, and source folder existence. Nothing has been mutated yet.
90	5. **Disk-space preflight.** Check free space on both hosts in parallel against `preflight_minimum`; abort if either is short — so snapshots and rsync don't run a disk into ENOSPC.
91	6. **Pre-sync snapshots.** Create btrfs snapshots on both hosts. This is the rollback point; every mutating step below happens after it.
92	7. **Install/upgrade pc-switcher on target.** Ensures the target has a compatible version to run its side of later jobs. After snapshots, so a bad install is recoverable.
93	8. **Sync config to target.** Copy this machine's config to the target (prompting on diff unless `--yes`), so both ends run jobs with identical settings.
94	9. **Run sync jobs sequentially.** The actual data movement, one UI step per enabled job. By default `folder_sync` (rsync-over-SSH as root on both ends) runs first, then `vscode_state_sync` (a selective, SQLite-aware merge of each editor's `state.vscdb` that keeps the target's machine-bound `secret://` keys). A background disk-space monitor runs concurrently and aborts the sync if free space crosses `runtime_minimum`. First job failure stops the run.
95	10. **Post-sync snapshots.** Snapshot both hosts again, capturing the synced state. (This logical phase is the last UI step — Step 11 with the default job set.)
96	
97	On success, the workflow records sync history on both machines, enabling the out-of-order check next time. Skipped in `--dry-run`.
98	
99	With `--dry-run`, the workflow previews without writing state (no history update, no snapshots, no mutations). rsync `--dry-run` lists the exact files and deletions that would occur; deletions are recorded in the FULL-level log so you can audit what would be destroyed before committing to a live sync. `--allow-out-of-order` skips the out-of-order / target-state confirmation.
100	
101	## Configuration
102	
103	Run `pc-switcher init` to write the default configuration to `~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml`. The generated file is annotated with inline comments for every setting.

I've mapped the full picture. There's a genuine fork in how to read your request, and it changes scope, so let me confirm before building.

Current state: there's **one logical numbering** shared by README + code comments (`# Step N`), and the **live UI numbers dynamically** (the run-jobs phase expands to one step per job — README documents this as the single intentional divergence). The two items you flagged — the out-of-order check and the history recording — are today written as *non-numbered* asides, and critically they also get **no UI progress step** (the out-of-order check comment literally says "no progress step of its own").

So "give it a step number and renumber the following" is clear for the docs/comments. What's ambiguous is whether these two should *also* become live UI progress steps (i.e. show up in the "Step X/Y" bar during a sync), or stay doc-level logical steps only.

input.old_string:
The ten logical phases are listed below. The live UI numbers steps dynamically, because the run-jobs phase expands to one step per enabled sync job. With the default two jobs (`folder_sync`, then `vscode_state_sync`), a sync runs as 11 steps, ending with the post-sync snapshots.

1. **Acquire source lock.** Local lock file; this machine cannot join any other sync (as source or target) while this one runs.
2. **Establish SSH connection.** Creates the local and remote executors every later step uses. Nothing touches the target before this point.
3. **Acquire target lock.** A persistent remote process holds the same unified lock on the target; released during cleanup.
   - *Between steps 3 and 4 (not a progress step): out-of-order check.* Detects cases where the target may hold independent state — no prior sync history, the target last synced with a different machine, or this machine pushing again without a back-sync first. Warns and asks for confirmation (never a hard abort, since re-syncing the same direction is a legitimate workflow). Skip with `--allow-out-of-order`; in `--dry-run` the warning is logged and the sync continues.
4. **Discover & validate jobs.**
   - Load enabled jobs from config
   - Validate their config
   - Run each job's `validate()` against live system state: checks `sudo rsync` availability, `acl` package installation, and source folder existence. Nothing has been mutated yet.
5. **Disk-space preflight.** Check free space on both hosts in parallel against `preflight_minimum`; abort if either is short — so snapshots and rsync don't run a disk into ENOSPC.
6. **Pre-sync snapshots.** Create btrfs snapshots on both hosts. This is the rollback point; every mutating step below happens after it.
7. **Install/upgrade pc-switcher on target.** Ensures the target has a compatible version to run its side of later jobs. After snapshots, so a bad install is recoverable.
8. **Sync config to target.** Copy this machine's config to the target (prompting on diff unless `--yes`), so both ends run jobs with identical settings.
9. **Run sync jobs sequentially.** The actual data movement, one UI step per enabled job. By default `folder_sync` (rsync-over-SSH as root on both ends) runs first, then `vscode_state_sync` (a selective, SQLite-aware merge of each editor's `state.vscdb` that keeps the target's machine-bound `secret://` keys). A background disk-space monitor runs concurrently and aborts the sync if free space crosses `runtime_minimum`. First job failure stops the run.
10. **Post-sync snapshots.** Snapshot both hosts again, capturing the synced state. (This logical phase is the last UI step — Step 11 with the default job set.)

On success, the workflow records sync history on both machines, enabling the out-of-order check next time. Skipped in `--dry-run`.

By default `folder_sync` (rsync-over-SSH as root on both ends) runs first, then `vscode_state_sync` (a selective, SQLite-aware merge of each editor's `state.vscdb` that keeps the target's machine-bound `secret://` keys). A background disk-space monitor runs concurrently and aborts the sync if free space crosses `runtime_minimum`. First job failure stops the run.
11. **Post-sync snapshots.** Snapshot both hosts again, capturing the synced state.

Done. Draft PR #197: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/pull/197

What I did, per your remark on #186 (full consistency for user + dev, UI included):

Promoted both formerly-unnumbered items to real steps across **all three surfaces** — README list, orchestrator `# Step N` comments, and the live `Step X/Y` bar:

- **Step 12** — record sync history (was the trailing "On success, ..." paragraph).

Sequence went 10 → 12 logical steps; the run-jobs step still expands per job, so with the default two jobs the UI now shows 13 steps. The history step advances the counter even under `--dry-run` (write still skipped), matching the existing snapshot-step behavior. Total-steps formula and every cross-reference bumped accordingly (job-discovery step 4→5, config-sync step 8→9 in docs).

Verified: ruff + basedpyright clean, 115 orchestrator/UI unit tests pass. No test asserted on the old concrete numbers, so nothing broke there. Left as draft so integration CI doesn't fire early.

One thing I did **not** touch: the illustrative `"Step 3/7: Package Sync"` example in `docs/system/core.md:312` — it's a hypothetical BDD example with a fictional job, not tied to the real sequence. Say the word if you want it aligned too.
